sections.title: Amazon EC2 Basics
points: Amazon EC2 stands for Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud.
EC2 provides on-demand virtual servers in AWS.
EC2 is more flexible, cost-effective, and faster to start than buying and managing on-premises servers.
You can launch instances in minutes instead of waiting for hardware purchase, delivery, and setup.
You pay for running compute capacity, which makes EC2 useful for workloads that change over time.
EC2 gives you control over the operating system, software, storage, and networking settings on the instance.
EC2 is considered an unmanaged compute service compared with more managed AWS options.
sections.exam_tip: If the question asks for virtual servers, customizable compute, or control over the OS, think EC2.

sections.title: How EC2 Compares to On-Premises
points: On-premises infrastructure requires upfront hardware purchases, installation, and maintenance.
On-premises capacity can be hard to predict, which leads to overbuying or underbuying hardware.
EC2 removes the delay of physical hardware setup.
EC2 lets you start small, scale up or down, and stop instances when you no longer need them.
This makes EC2 more agile than traditional on-premises servers.
sections.exam_tip: If AWS asks what EC2 improves over on-premises, the answer usually includes flexibility, speed, and cost-effectiveness.

sections.title: EC2 Instances, Virtual Machines, and Multi-Tenancy
points: EC2 instances are virtual machines (VMs).
Multiple EC2 instances can share an underlying physical host machine.
This sharing model is called multi-tenancy.
Even though instances share a host, they are isolated from one another.
The software that manages this sharing and isolation is called a hypervisor.
AWS manages the host hardware, the hypervisor, and the isolation between instances.
sections.exam_tip: Multi-tenancy does NOT mean all workloads mix together. It means shared host, isolated virtual machines.

sections.title: What You Control on an EC2 Instance
points: You choose the operating system, such as Linux or Windows.
You choose what software runs on the instance.
You can configure internal business apps, websites, databases, or third-party software.
You can choose networking behavior, such as whether the instance is publicly or privately accessible.
You can resize the instance if the workload needs more memory or CPU.
sections.exam_tip: If the question mentions customer control over the guest operating system or installed software, that fits EC2.

sections.title: Scaling Concepts in EC2
points: Vertical scaling means making one instance bigger by adding more CPU or memory.
Horizontal scaling means adding more instances instead of making one instance bigger.
Vertical scaling is also called scaling up.
Horizontal scaling is also called scaling out.
Horizontal scaling is often better when more work needs to happen in parallel.
sections.exam_tip: Bigger machine = vertical scaling. More machines = horizontal scaling.

sections.title: EC2 Instance Families
points: General purpose instances provide a balanced mix of compute, memory, and networking.
Compute optimized instances are best for CPU-intensive tasks like high-performance computing and scientific modeling.
Memory optimized instances are best for memory-heavy workloads like large in-memory datasets and databases.
Accelerated computing instances use hardware accelerators such as GPUs.
Storage optimized instances are best for workloads needing high local storage performance and high disk throughput.
sections.exam_tip: Balanced = general purpose. CPU-heavy = compute optimized. RAM-heavy = memory optimized. GPU = accelerated. Disk-heavy = storage optimized.

sections.title: Choosing the Right Instance Type
points: General purpose is often the safest starting point when you do not yet know the workload behavior.
Compute optimized is best when the processor is the bottleneck.
Memory optimized is best when the workload must process large datasets in RAM.
Storage optimized is best when performance depends on fast local data access.
Accelerated computing is best for graphics, floating point calculations, and machine learning workloads.
sections.exam_tip: AWS exam questions love asking which instance family is BEST. Focus on what the workload is heavy on: CPU, memory, GPU, or storage.

sections.title: Ways to Interact with AWS Resources
points: Everything in AWS is ultimately an API call.
You can interact with AWS using the AWS Management Console, AWS CLI, or AWS SDK.
The AWS Management Console is browser-based and good for visual management and getting started.
The AWS CLI is command-line based and supports automation and scripting.
The AWS SDK lets developers work with AWS services using programming languages such as Python, Java, and others.
sections.exam_tip: Console = visual. CLI = automation/scripts. SDK = use AWS inside code.

sections.title: Console vs CLI vs SDK
points: The Management Console is good for learning, quick setup, billing checks, and visual workflows.
The CLI reduces repetitive manual steps and is better for automation.
The SDK is best when embedding AWS operations into applications.
Manual console work can increase the chance of human error in repeat tasks.
AWS CLI and SDK are better choices for repeatable and automated provisioning.
sections.exam_tip: If the question asks about reducing manual steps or scripting, the best answer is usually AWS CLI.

sections.title: Shared Responsibility and EC2
points: AWS secures the cloud infrastructure, including hardware and facilities.
The customer secures what runs inside the EC2 instance.
With EC2, the customer manages the guest operating system, applications, updates, and instance security settings.
The customer is also responsible for configuring firewalls such as security groups.
This is why EC2 gives more control but also more responsibility.
sections.exam_tip: For EC2, AWS handles the hardware. You handle the OS, apps, patches, and access.

sections.title: Launching an EC2 Instance
points: When launching an EC2 instance, you choose a name for the instance.
You choose an Amazon Machine Image (AMI), which acts as a template.
You choose an instance type, which determines CPU, memory, and other capacity.
You configure a key pair for secure login access.
You configure network settings, such as allowing HTTP access for a web server.
You configure storage, such as attaching an EBS volume.
You can also provide user data scripts to install and configure software automatically at launch.
sections.exam_tip: Core launch ideas: AMI, instance type, storage, networking, key pair.

sections.title: What an AMI Does
points: AMI stands for Amazon Machine Image.
An AMI is a template used when launching EC2 instances.
It includes the operating system and can include built-in software and settings.
An AMI helps create repeatable deployments because multiple instances can launch from the same template.
You can build your own AMI, use AWS-provided AMIs, or buy AMIs from AWS Marketplace.
sections.exam_tip: AMI = template. It does not choose size or networking by itself. It pre-configures the OS and software.

sections.title: EC2 Pricing Models
points: On-Demand pricing means you pay only for the time your instance runs, with no long-term commitment.
Savings Plans give discounts in exchange for a usage commitment over one or three years.
Reserved Instances are good for predictable, steady-state workloads and can reduce cost significantly.
Spot Instances use spare AWS capacity and can be interrupted by AWS.
Dedicated Hosts give you an entire physical server for your exclusive use.
Dedicated Instances also provide hardware isolation, but without full control over physical server placement.
sections.exam_tip: No commitment = On-Demand. Predictable long-term usage = Reserved Instances or Savings Plans. Interruptible cheap workloads = Spot.

sections.title: When to Use Each EC2 Pricing Option
points: On-Demand is best when you are just getting started or your usage is unpredictable.
Savings Plans are best when you expect steady compute usage across services and want flexible discounts.
Reserved Instances are best for predictable workloads with long-term commitment.
Spot Instances are best for fault-tolerant workloads that can handle interruption.
Dedicated Hosts are best for strict compliance, licensing, or full control over physical placement.
sections.exam_tip: If the question says 'can tolerate interruptions' → Spot. If it says 'no long-term commitment' → On-Demand.

sections.title: Dedicated Hosts vs Dedicated Instances
points: Dedicated Hosts give exclusive use of an entire physical server.
Dedicated Hosts allow more control over instance placement and host-level visibility.
Dedicated Instances give isolation at the account level but without choosing the exact physical server.
Both are about isolation, but Dedicated Hosts give more control.
sections.exam_tip: Need full control over the physical server itself? Dedicated Hosts.

sections.title: Scalability vs Elasticity
points: Scalability is the ability to handle growth by adding resources.
Elasticity is the ability to automatically grow and shrink resources in response to real-time demand.
Scalability often refers to long-term growth planning.
Elasticity is more about dynamic reaction to current demand.
sections.exam_tip: Growth over time = scalability. Automatic up/down based on demand = elasticity.

sections.title: Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling
points: EC2 Auto Scaling automatically adjusts the number of EC2 instances based on demand.
Dynamic scaling reacts in real time to traffic or metric changes.
Predictive scaling tries to prepare capacity in advance based on expected demand.
Auto Scaling improves both cost efficiency and availability.
Auto Scaling works by scaling out when demand rises and scaling in when demand falls.
sections.exam_tip: If the question mentions fluctuating demand and avoiding over-provisioning, think EC2 Auto Scaling.

sections.title: Minimum, Desired, and Maximum Capacity
points: Minimum capacity is the smallest number of instances that should always be running.
Desired capacity is the target number of instances Auto Scaling tries to maintain.
Maximum capacity is the upper limit so scaling does not go too far and create unnecessary cost.
If desired capacity is not specified, it defaults to the minimum capacity.
sections.exam_tip: Desired = target. Minimum = never go below. Maximum = do not exceed.

sections.title: Elastic Load Balancing (ELB)
points: Elastic Load Balancing distributes incoming traffic across multiple EC2 instances.
ELB prevents one instance from becoming overloaded while others sit idle.
ELB improves scalability and reliability by balancing traffic automatically.
ELB and Auto Scaling work well together.
As instances are added or removed, the load balancer continues routing traffic to healthy instances.
sections.exam_tip: Auto Scaling changes the number of instances. ELB distributes traffic across them.

sections.title: Why ELB Matters
points: ELB acts as a single point of contact for incoming traffic.
ELB decouples the front end from the back end.
Front-end services do not need to know every backend instance individually.
ELB simplifies failover, maintenance, and traffic routing.
This design makes applications easier to scale and maintain.
sections.exam_tip: If the question is about distributing traffic, the answer is ELB, not Auto Scaling.

sections.title: Routing Methods Mentioned
points: Round Robin sends traffic evenly across servers in order.
Least Connections sends traffic to the server with the fewest active connections.
IP Hash uses the client IP to route traffic consistently to the same server.
Least Response Time sends traffic to the server responding fastest.
sections.exam_tip: You probably will not need every routing method in detail for Cloud Practitioner, but know ELB uses routing strategies to balance traffic.

sections.title: Messaging and Queuing Basics
points: Applications often need to communicate with one another.
If they depend on each other directly, the system is tightly coupled.
Tightly coupled systems are more fragile because one failure can break other parts.
A queue acts like a buffer between services.
Using a queue creates a more loosely coupled architecture.
sections.exam_tip: Direct dependency = tightly coupled. Queue/buffer between services = loosely coupled.

sections.title: Amazon SQS
points: Amazon SQS is a message queuing service.
It lets applications send, store, and receive messages at any scale.
Messages remain in the queue until they are processed.
This means one service can continue sending messages even if another service is temporarily down.
SQS improves reliability and loose coupling.
sections.exam_tip: If the question says messages should wait until a service is available, the answer is SQS.

sections.title: Amazon SNS
points: Amazon SNS is a publish-subscribe messaging service.
SNS is used to send messages to multiple subscribers.
Subscribers can include email, SMS, Lambda functions, web servers, and more.
SNS is useful for fan-out notifications and near-immediate message delivery.
SNS is different from SQS because SNS is not mainly about storing messages for later pickup.
sections.exam_tip: SQS = queue and hold. SNS = publish and push.

sections.title: Amazon EventBridge
points: EventBridge is an event-driven service that routes events from sources to targets.
It works with AWS services, custom applications, and third-party sources.
EventBridge helps build scalable event-driven systems.
It can filter, transform, and route events automatically.
sections.exam_tip: If the question is about events triggering actions across services, think EventBridge.

sections.title: Tightly Coupled vs Loosely Coupled
points: Tightly coupled components depend directly on each other.
If one tightly coupled component fails, it can affect the others.
Loosely coupled components can continue operating even if one component fails.
Queues and event-driven designs help create loosely coupled systems.
Microservices architectures often use loose coupling for resilience and flexibility.
sections.exam_tip: AWS generally prefers loosely coupled architectures because they are more resilient.

sections.title: Monolithic vs Microservices
points: A monolithic application has tightly connected components.
Failure in one part of a monolithic app can affect the whole application.
Microservices split the application into smaller, loosely coupled parts.
Microservices improve flexibility, scalability, and fault isolation.
sections.exam_tip: Monolith = tightly coupled. Microservices = loosely coupled.

sections.title: Fast Memory Anchors
points: EC2 = virtual servers.
AMI = template.
CLI = automation.
On-Demand = no commitment.
Spot = cheapest but interruptible.
Auto Scaling = change instance count automatically.
ELB = spread traffic.
SQS = queue and hold messages.
SNS = publish and push notifications.
EventBridge = route events.
sections.exam_tip: If you only memorize one block from this module, memorize this one.

sections.title: Most Common Exam Traps in This Module
points: Auto Scaling is not the same as ELB. Auto Scaling changes capacity; ELB distributes traffic.
SQS is not the same as SNS. SQS stores messages; SNS pushes notifications.
On-Demand is not the same as Spot. On-Demand has no commitment; Spot can be interrupted.
General purpose is not the same as compute optimized. General purpose is balanced; compute optimized is CPU-focused.
CLI is not the same as Console. CLI is for automation; Console is visual.
sections.exam_tip: AWS loves asking questions that test whether you can separate similar services.
